subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 32-year-old man is found to have killed his neighbor during a sudden quarrel without any premeditation. The act was done in the heat of passion without the use of any weapon. Under which IPC section would this act most appropriately be charged?
explanation: Section 304 IPC deals with culpable homicide not amounting to murder. This applies when death is caused without the intention to cause death or knowledge that death is likely, or when caused in the heat of passion (Exception 1 to Section 300). Section 302 is for murder (culpable homicide with full intention). Section 304A is for death by negligence. Section 307 is for attempt to murder.
highYield: Distinction between Sec 302 (murder/intention) and Sec 304 (culpable homicide not amounting to murder/heat of passion or provocation) is a classic NEET PG question.
pyqNote: This scenario-based distinction has appeared in multiple NEET PG and AIIMS PG papers.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A doctor performs an emergency operation on an unconscious patient brought to the ER without relatives present. The patient later dies. The family threatens legal action. Under which IPC section is the doctor protected?
explanation: Section 92 IPC protects acts done in good faith for the benefit of a person without their consent, when consent cannot be obtained due to the person being unconscious or incapable. This applies to emergency medical interventions. Section 88 protects acts with consent. Section 90 defines invalid consent. Section 84 pertains to the defense of unsound mind.
highYield: Section 92 is the 'good Samaritan/emergency medical care' section — frequently tested in NEET PG forensic medicine.
pyqNote: Direct one-liner questions on this section appear almost every year.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 25-year-old woman is allegedly raped by her employer who threatened to terminate her job if she did not comply. She consented under this fear. What is the legal status of this consent under IPC?
explanation: Section 90 IPC states that consent given under fear of injury, or under a misconception of fact, is not valid consent. Since the woman consented under fear of job loss (fear of injury to her livelihood), this consent is legally invalid. This is directly relevant to sexual offenses where consent obtained by coercion, threat, or fraud is treated as no consent.
highYield: Section 90 defines when consent is vitiated — critical for rape, assault, and medical ethics questions in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Scenario-based questions testing Section 90 vs valid consent appear frequently.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 28-year-old bride is found dead under suspicious circumstances 5 years after her marriage. Autopsy reveals burns. Her in-laws had been demanding more dowry and subjecting her to cruelty. Which section of IPC primarily governs this offense?
explanation: Section 304B IPC (Dowry Death) applies when death of a woman occurs within 7 years of marriage and is caused by burns, bodily injury, or under suspicious circumstances, soon before which she was subjected to cruelty or harassment by husband/in-laws in connection with dowry demands. Key elements: within 7 years of marriage + dowry demand + cruelty/harassment. Section 302 is for murder with intention. Section 304 is culpable homicide. Section 306 is abetment of suicide.
highYield: Section 304B — three criteria: within 7 years of marriage, dowry demand, and cruelty — is extremely high yield for NEET PG.
pyqNote: Dowry death IPC section with time frame (7 years) is a frequently tested direct question.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A gynecologist is charged for causing the death of a pregnant patient during an abortion procedure due to reckless handling and failure to follow standard precautions. Under which IPC section is she most likely to be charged?
explanation: Section 304A IPC deals with causing death by a rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide. It applies when there is no intention to cause death but death results from gross negligence or rashness. This is the primary section for cases of medical negligence causing death. Section 302 requires intention to murder. Section 315 deals with preventing a child from being born alive.
highYield: Section 304A is THE section for medical negligence causing death — most commonly tested forensic IPC section in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Jacob Mathew vs State of Punjab case is based on Section 304A. Direct and scenario questions appear regularly.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A husband repeatedly insults, mentally tortures, and isolates his wife from her family. The wife, unable to bear the harassment, hangs herself. The husband is arrested. Under which IPC section will he primarily be charged?
explanation: Section 306 IPC deals with abetment of suicide. If a person abets (encourages, instigates, or aids) another person to commit suicide, they are liable under this section. Mental torture and isolation leading to suicide constitute abetment. Note: if death occurs within 7 years of marriage with dowry demands, Section 304B would also apply. Here, the scenario fits 306 primarily as there is no mention of dowry.
highYield: Section 306 (abetment of suicide) vs 304B (dowry death within 7 years) distinction is a common NEET PG trap.
pyqNote: Differentiation between 304B and 306 in domestic harassment scenarios is a recurring exam pattern.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man fires a gun at another person with the intent to kill but misses and the victim survives. The attacker is arrested. Under which IPC section is he primarily charged?
explanation: Section 307 IPC covers attempt to murder — when a person does any act with the intention or knowledge that if the death is caused, it would be murder. Since the person had the intention to kill (aimed to murder), but failed, this is Section 307. Section 308 is for attempt to commit culpable homicide (lesser intent). Section 302 requires actual death. Section 304 requires actual death without full murderous intent.
highYield: Section 307 (attempt to murder) vs Section 308 (attempt to commit culpable homicide) depends on the intent level — key distinction for NEET PG.
pyqNote: Attempt scenarios with surviving victims frequently appear in forensic MCQs.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man charged with a violent offense claims he committed the act while having a severe psychotic episode with no understanding of the nature of his actions or that they were wrong. Which legal principle and IPC section provides his defense?
explanation: Section 84 IPC provides that nothing is an offense done by a person of unsound mind who is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that what they are doing is wrong or contrary to law. This is based on the McNaughton Rules (1843): the accused must be laboring under such a defect of reason from disease of mind as not to know the nature of the act, or that it was wrong. Section 92 is for good faith acts. Section 85 deals with intoxication.
highYield: McNaughton Rules + Section 84 IPC = the insanity defense. Definition, limitations, and case applications are all high yield.
pyqNote: The exact wording of McNaughton rules and its IPC section number appear as direct questions in NEET PG.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A doctor terminates a pregnancy in a woman without her consent claiming it was necessary to save her life. Under which IPC section would this act be examined if a complaint is filed?
explanation: Section 313 IPC deals specifically with causing miscarriage WITHOUT the consent of the woman. This is a more serious offense than Section 312 (causing miscarriage with consent). Section 315 is for acts done to prevent a child from being born alive or to cause death after birth. Section 316 is for causing death of a quick unborn child. The absence of consent makes this Section 313.
highYield: The consent criterion distinguishes Section 312 (with consent) from Section 313 (without consent) — a classic one-liner question.
pyqNote: Miscarriage IPC sections (312-316) are tested as a group. The consent factor in 313 is the most commonly tested point.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A traditional birth attendant performs a maneuver during delivery that results in the newborn being born dead. There was no intent to harm but the act prevented the child from being born alive. Which IPC section applies?
explanation: Section 315 IPC covers acts done with intent to prevent a child from being born alive, or to cause it to die after birth. This includes acts during delivery that intentionally or negligently prevent live birth. Section 316 covers causing the death of a quick unborn child by acts amounting to culpable homicide. Section 312 is for miscarriage with consent. The key here is the act prevented live birth.
highYield: Section 315 specifically relates to perinatal acts preventing live birth — distinct from miscarriage sections.
pyqNote: Differentiation between sections 312, 313, 315, and 316 based on stage (antenatal vs perinatal) and consent appears in NEET PG.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man attacks another with acid, causing permanent disfigurement of the face. Under Section 320 IPC, this constitutes grievous hurt. Which specific type of grievous hurt does this represent?
options: Permanent disfigurement of the face or head
explanation: Section 320 IPC defines 8 types of grievous hurt. Permanent disfigurement of the face or head is specifically one of them (type 6). All 8 types: (1) Emasculation, (2) Permanent privation of sight of either eye, (3) Permanent privation of hearing of either ear, (4) Privation of any member or joint, (5) Destruction or permanent impairing of powers of any member or joint, (6) Permanent disfigurement of face or head, (7) Fracture or dislocation of bone or tooth, (8) Any hurt endangering life or causing 20-day severe illness/incapacitation.
highYield: All 8 types of grievous hurt under Section 320 are extremely high yield — memorize the complete list.
pyqNote: NEET PG frequently asks to identify which type of grievous hurt applies to a given scenario, or to state the total number of types.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: How many types of grievous hurt are defined under Section 320 of the IPC?
explanation: Section 320 IPC defines exactly 8 types of grievous hurt: (1) Emasculation, (2) Permanent loss of sight of either eye, (3) Permanent loss of hearing of either ear, (4) Loss of member or joint, (5) Destruction/permanent impairing of any member or joint, (6) Permanent disfigurement of face or head, (7) Fracture or dislocation of bone or tooth, (8) Any hurt endangering life or causing severe incapacitation for 20 days or more.
highYield: The number '8' types of grievous hurt is a direct one-liner question in NEET PG.
pyqNote: This has appeared as a direct numerical question in multiple NEET PG papers.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man is attacked and sustains a fracture of his tibia. He is incapacitated and bedridden for 25 days. Under Section 320 IPC, which category/categories of grievous hurt are applicable?
options: Both fracture/dislocation of bone AND hurt causing 20-day incapacitation
explanation: Under Section 320 IPC, a tibial fracture satisfies TWO categories of grievous hurt simultaneously: (7) Fracture or dislocation of a bone or tooth, AND (8) Any hurt which endangers life or which causes the sufferer to be in severe bodily pain or unable to follow his ordinary pursuits for 20 days. Since incapacitation exceeds 20 days (25 days here), both clauses apply. In practice, the fracture alone is sufficient to constitute grievous hurt.
highYield: The 20-day threshold for incapacitation under Section 320 is a high-yield numerical fact for NEET PG.
pyqNote: Questions on the 20-day threshold and dual applicability of Section 320 appear in forensic MCQs.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 16-year-old girl reports sexual abuse to her school teacher. The teacher decides not to report the matter to police to 'protect' the girl's reputation. Under POCSO Act, what is the teacher's legal obligation and what offense has been committed?
options: No legal obligation; teacher acted correctly
Mandatory reporting obligation violated; teacher has committed an offense
Optional reporting; teacher may report at discretion
Reporting required only if physical injury is present
explanation: The POCSO Act (Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012) mandates reporting of any sexual offence against a child (person under 18 years). Section 19 of POCSO imposes a mandatory duty on any person who has knowledge of an offence to report it to the police or Special Court. Failure to report is itself an offense under Section 21, punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months and/or fine. The law applies regardless of injury, consent, or reputation concerns.
highYield: POCSO mandatory reporting (Section 19) and penalty for non-reporting (Section 21) are high-yield points in NEET PG forensic questions.
pyqNote: POCSO provisions — age criterion, mandatory reporting, in-camera trial — appear as direct MCQs.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Under the POCSO Act 2012, what is the age threshold below which a person is considered a 'child' for the purposes of the Act?
explanation: The POCSO Act 2012 defines a 'child' as any person below the age of 18 years. The Act protects all children under 18 regardless of gender from sexual assault, sexual harassment, and pornography. Key features include: mandatory reporting, in-camera trials, child-friendly procedures, and Special Courts for trial. Age of consent under POCSO is 18 years.
highYield: Age criterion under POCSO (under 18 years) is a direct one-liner question in NEET PG forensic medicine.
pyqNote: Direct question on POCSO age threshold appears annually in medical entrance exams.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: After the 2013 Criminal Law Amendment Act, Section 375 IPC was revised. Which of the following acts now constitutes rape under the revised definition?
options: Penetration of vagina, urethra, or anus by penis, object, or body part; or oral sex — all without consent
Only acts causing bodily injury along with penetration
Only penetration by penis with intention to harm
explanation: Post the Criminal Law Amendment Act 2013 (Nirbhaya Act), Section 375 IPC was significantly broadened. Rape now includes: (a) penile penetration of vagina/urethra/anus/mouth, (b) insertion of object or body part into vagina/urethra/anus of a woman, (c) manipulation of any body part of a woman for penetration, (d) oral sex — all without consent or with vitiated consent. The definition also raised the age of consent to 18 years. Absence of injury does not negate rape.
highYield: The expanded post-2013 definition of rape under Section 375 is extremely high yield — know all four categories.
pyqNote: Questions on expanded definition of rape post-Nirbhaya Act are frequently tested in NEET PG.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man is accused of sexual activity with another male adult. Under which IPC section was this previously prosecuted in India, and what was the landmark judgment that decriminalized consensual same-sex acts between adults?
options: Section 375; Navtej Singh Johar vs Union of India 2018
Section 377; Navtej Singh Johar vs Union of India 2018
Section 377; Naz Foundation vs Government of NCT of Delhi 2009
explanation: Section 377 IPC historically criminalized 'carnal intercourse against the order of nature' — broadly covering homosexual acts. In Navtej Singh Johar vs Union of India (2018), the Supreme Court of India (Constitution Bench) decriminalized consensual same-sex acts between consenting adults by reading down Section 377. Section 377 still applies to non-consensual acts and acts with minors. The Naz Foundation case (2009) was the Delhi HC judgment, subsequently reversed by the Supreme Court in Koushal (2013) before the final 2018 ruling.
highYield: Section 377 IPC + Navtej Singh Johar 2018 judgment is a high-yield forensic law question.
pyqNote: Legal evolution of Section 377 and landmark judgments are tested in NEET PG and PGI papers.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 45-year-old man is on mechanical ventilation following a massive head injury. The neurologist evaluates him for brain death. Which of the following is NOT a prerequisite (precondition) before conducting brain death testing?
options: Establishment of an irreversible cause of coma
Exclusion of reversible causes such as hypothermia, drug intoxication, and metabolic derangements
Presence of a known etiology causing the coma
Completion of organ donation consent forms
explanation: Before conducting brain death testing, three preconditions must be established: (1) Coma of known etiology — the cause must be identified and be sufficient to explain the clinical picture, (2) Irreversibility — the condition must be irreversible, and (3) Exclusion of reversible causes — hypothermia (temperature must be >35°C), drug intoxication, metabolic disturbances, and neuromuscular blockade must be excluded. Organ donation consent is a separate legal/ethical step and is NOT a precondition for brain death testing itself.
highYield: The three preconditions for brain death testing are high yield — etiology known + irreversible + reversible causes excluded.
pyqNote: Brain death preconditions and clinical tests appear regularly in NEET PG and AIIMS PG.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: During brain death evaluation, the apnea test is being performed. What is the target arterial PaCO2 level that must be reached (in the absence of chronic CO2 retention) to declare the apnea test positive?
options: PaCO2 > 45 mmHg with no respiratory effort
PaCO2 > 60 mmHg (or rise of 20 mmHg from baseline) with no respiratory effort
PaCO2 > 50 mmHg with no respiratory effort
PaCO2 > 55 mmHg with no respiratory effort
explanation: The apnea test for brain death requires disconnecting the ventilator (while providing passive oxygenation via catheter) and observing for any spontaneous respiratory effort. A positive apnea test (supporting brain death) requires PaCO2 to rise to >60 mmHg (or 20 mmHg above baseline in patients with chronic CO2 retention) with NO spontaneous respiratory effort throughout the observation period. This confirms absence of medullary respiratory drive, a critical brainstem function.
highYield: PaCO2 >60 mmHg threshold in the apnea test for brain death confirmation is a frequently tested numerical value.
pyqNote: The apnea test threshold (PaCO2 >60 mmHg) appears in NEET PG, AIIMS PG, and PGI forensic papers.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 30-year-old woman survives a cardiac arrest but is in a state where she has sleep-wake cycles, eye opening, no awareness, no meaningful response to commands, and no cortical function. Brainstem reflexes are intact. What is this condition?
explanation: Persistent Vegetative State (PVS) is characterized by: preserved brainstem function (including sleep-wake cycles, eye opening, autonomic functions), complete loss of awareness and cognitive function, no purposeful response, but intact brainstem reflexes. This distinguishes it from Brain Death (complete cessation of all brain function including brainstem; no sleep-wake cycles; apnea; no brainstem reflexes). Locked-in syndrome has preserved consciousness but motor paralysis. Coma has no eye opening.
highYield: PVS vs Brain Death is a classic NEET PG distinction — key: brainstem function present in PVS, absent in brain death.
pyqNote: Aruna Shanbaug case (PVS) brought this distinction to clinical and legal prominence in India.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A body is discovered 10 hours after death. On examination, there is bluish-purple discoloration on the dependent parts of the body. When you press on these areas, they do NOT blanch. What does this indicate?
options: Livor mortis that has not yet fixed — body was moved
Fixed livor mortis — body has NOT been moved after 6-12 hours
explanation: Livor mortis (hypostasis) begins 1-2 hours after death as blood settles to dependent parts. It is initially unfixed (blanches on pressure) for the first 6-12 hours, then becomes fixed (does not blanch) after 6-12 hours as blood undergoes hemolysis and staining. Non-blanching at 10 hours indicates fixation. Fixed livor at the site confirms the body has not been moved since fixation. Discordance between livor pattern and body position indicates the body was moved after fixation — useful in crime scene analysis.
highYield: Livor mortis fixation at 6-12 hours, blanching vs non-blanching, and its medicolegal significance are all high yield.
pyqNote: Livor mortis timing and fixation are tested directly and in crime scene scenario questions in NEET PG.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A forensic pathologist examines a body and notes that rigor mortis is fully established in the jaw and neck but not yet present in the lower limbs. Which law governs this sequence, and what is the approximate time since death?
options: Nysten's Rule; approximately 2-4 hours after death
Nysten's Rule; approximately 6-12 hours after death
Casper's Rule; approximately 12-24 hours after death
Watson's Rule; approximately 2-4 hours after death
explanation: Nysten's Rule describes the sequence of rigor mortis onset: jaw first → neck → trunk → upper limbs → lower limbs (descending order). Rigor mortis typically starts 2-4 hours after death, with full establishment (from jaw to lower limbs) occurring over 6-12 hours. The scenario describes early rigor (jaw + neck only, not lower limbs) suggesting 2-4 hours since death. Rigor mortis passes off in the same order (jaw first) at 36-48 hours. Casper's Rule applies to decomposition rates in different environments.
highYield: Nysten's Rule sequence (jaw to lower limbs) + timing of rigor mortis onset and resolution are extremely high yield.
pyqNote: Nysten's Rule and rigor mortis timing are among the most frequently tested postmortem change topics in NEET PG.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: At what approximate time after death does rigor mortis completely pass off (resolve)?
explanation: Rigor mortis follows a predictable timeline: onset at 2-4 hours (jaw first per Nysten's Rule), complete establishment by 6-12 hours, resolution/passing off by 36-48 hours. Resolution occurs in the same order as onset (jaw first). Beyond 48 hours, the body enters secondary flaccidity. Cadaveric spasm (instantaneous rigor) is an exception that has no relaxation phase before true rigor sets in.
highYield: Rigor mortis resolution at 36-48 hours is a direct one-liner question in NEET PG forensic medicine.
pyqNote: This specific timing fact appears frequently in direct one-liner MCQs.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A body recovered after 30 hours shows green discoloration of the abdomen. Which region shows this change first, and what is the responsible organism?
options: Right iliac fossa; Clostridium welchii (Clostridium perfringens)
explanation: The first sign of putrefaction is green discoloration of the right iliac fossa, appearing approximately 24 hours after death in tropical climates. The right iliac fossa is affected first because the cecum (which contains the highest concentration of gas-producing bacteria, predominantly Clostridium welchii/perfringens) is located here. These anaerobes produce hydrogen sulfide which reacts with hemoglobin to form sulfhemoglobin, causing the green discoloration. Subsequently, marbling, bloating, and advanced decomposition follow.
highYield: Right iliac fossa + Clostridium welchii as the first site/cause of putrefaction is a classic NEET PG high-yield fact.
pyqNote: This is one of the most frequently asked postmortem change questions in NEET PG forensic medicine.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A body is recovered from a waterlogged grave after several months. It has a greyish-white, soap-like consistency with a rancid odor. Soft tissues have been converted to a firm, waxy substance. What postmortem change is this?
explanation: Adipocere (saponification) is the conversion of body fat into a soap-like substance (hydroxy fatty acids/lithospermic acid). It occurs in warm, moist, anaerobic conditions (wet environments like waterlogged graves, ponds). It develops over weeks to months. It produces a greyish-white, waxy, rancid-smelling material that can preserve the body shape. Mummification, in contrast, occurs in hot, dry, well-ventilated environments where dehydration preserves the body. Key distinction: adipocere = wet/moist; mummification = dry/hot.
highYield: Adipocere formation conditions (warm + moist + anaerobic) vs mummification (dry + hot) is a classic distinction in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Adipocere description with environmental conditions is a frequently tested postmortem change question.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Algor mortis (postmortem cooling) occurs at what approximate rate during the first 6 hours after death in a temperate environment?
explanation: Algor mortis (postmortem cooling) proceeds at approximately 1°C per hour during the first 6 hours after death under standard conditions (ambient temperature ~20°C, average-sized adult, indoors). After the first 6 hours, the rate slows. The Henssge nomogram accounts for body weight and environmental temperature to more accurately estimate time since death using core body temperature. Obese individuals cool more slowly; thin individuals and children cool faster.
highYield: The cooling rate of 1°C/hour for the first 6 hours is a direct numerical fact tested in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Algor mortis rate appears as a direct one-liner in forensic medicine MCQs.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: At autopsy, a wound is found on the scalp. On examination: the edges are irregular and contused, hair roots are intact within the wound margins, tissue bridges (bridging) are present across the wound, and there is minimal bleeding. What type of wound is this?
explanation: A lacerated wound (caused by blunt force) has characteristic features: (1) Irregular, ragged, contused edges, (2) Tissue bridging across the wound (nerves, vessels, connective tissue intact across wound), (3) Hair roots intact within the wound margins, (4) Minimal bleeding compared to incised wounds, (5) Underlying bone may show fracture. An incised wound (sharp weapon) has clean, well-defined edges, no bridging, no intact hair roots, and profuse bleeding. Stab wounds have defined entry with depth greater than length.
highYield: Tissue bridging + intact hair roots = lacerated wound. This is the most classic wound type distinction in NEET PG forensic MCQs.
pyqNote: The distinguishing features of lacerated vs incised wounds are tested in almost every NEET PG forensic paper.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A victim is found with a long, clean-edged wound on the forearm. The edges are well-defined, clean, and everted. There is profuse bleeding. The wound is longer than it is deep. Which type of wound is this, and what is the typical weapon?
options: Lacerated wound caused by blunt object
Incised wound caused by sharp-edged weapon
Stab wound caused by sharp pointed weapon
explanation: An incised wound is caused by a sharp-edged weapon (knife, razor, glass). Features: (1) Clean, well-defined, everted edges — no contusion, (2) Length greater than depth, (3) Profuse bleeding (vessels cut cleanly), (4) No tissue bridging, (5) No intact hair roots at margins. Incised wounds on the forearm in suicide attempts typically show multiple parallel 'hesitation cuts.' A stab wound has depth greater than length. A lacerated wound has irregular edges with bridging.
highYield: Features of incised wound — clean edges, no bridging, profuse bleeding, length > depth — are core NEET PG knowledge.
pyqNote: Incised wound characteristics and typical weapons appear in wound classification questions in forensic MCQs.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 35-year-old man is stabbed with a single-edged knife. The entry wound is examined at autopsy. What are the characteristic features of this stab wound's entry site?
options: One sharp angle and one blunt/squared angle; depth greater than length of wound
Both angles are sharp; depth equals length of wound
Irregular edges with bridging; depth less than length
Cruciate shape indicating a double-edged weapon
explanation: A stab wound from a single-edged knife has an entry wound with: (1) One sharp angle (corresponding to the cutting edge) and one blunt/squared angle (corresponding to the blunt back of the blade), (2) Depth of wound GREATER than its length/width (this is the defining feature of stab wounds), (3) Clean edges without contusion or bridging. A double-edged knife produces a wound with both angles sharp (spindle-shaped wound). A cruciate wound suggests a Phillips screwdriver or other specialized weapon. Length greater than depth = incised wound.
highYield: Single-edged vs double-edged knife wound characteristics (sharp angles) and depth > length are highly tested in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Stab wound angle analysis and determination of weapon type from entry wound shape are classic forensic MCQ formats.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: In a stab wound, the width of the skin entry wound corresponds to which dimension of the responsible weapon?
options: Breadth (width) of the blade at its widest point
explanation: In stab wounds, the breadth (width) of the entry wound corresponds to the breadth of the weapon blade at its widest point. The length of the wound corresponds to the breadth of the blade. The depth of the wound corresponds to the depth of penetration (which may be greater than the blade length if the flesh is compressed). The entry wound is typically shorter than the actual blade due to skin elasticity retraction post-withdrawal. Understanding this relationship allows forensic reconstruction of the weapon from wound characteristics.
highYield: The relationship between stab wound dimensions and weapon dimensions (breadth of wound = breadth of blade) is a core forensic concept.
pyqNote: Weapon reconstruction from stab wound measurements appears in NEET PG and forensic practical examinations.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A murder victim is found lying on their back with livor mortis fixed on the back. However, there is also unexpected lividity on the anterior (front) of the body. Which of the following best explains this finding?
options: The victim was buried face down initially, confirming the body was not moved
The body was moved from a face-down position to the current face-up position AFTER livor mortis fixation (6-12 hours)
The anterior lividity is due to putrefaction, not livor mortis
Double lividity never occurs and this finding is artifactual
explanation: When a body is moved after livor mortis has fixed (>6-12 hours), the original pattern of lividity remains at its fixed location (here: face down = anterior surface). New lividity then begins to form at the new dependent surfaces (here: back, after body was turned face up). This results in double/paradoxical lividity — lividity at sites not consistent with current position. This is a critical medicolegal finding indicating the body was repositioned after death, suggesting crime scene staging.
highYield: Paradoxical or double lividity indicating body repositioning after death is a high-yield forensic investigation topic.
pyqNote: Crime scene reconstruction using paradoxical lividity appears in scenario-based NEET PG forensic questions.

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man assaults another with a sharp weapon, severing his dominant hand at the wrist. Under Section 320 IPC, which specific type of grievous hurt does this constitute?
options: Privation of any member or joint
Permanent disfigurement of face or head
explanation: Loss (privation) of any member or joint is the 4th type of grievous hurt under Section 320 IPC. Amputation of the hand at the wrist joint constitutes privation of a member (the hand) and a joint (wrist). Note the distinction from type 5 (destruction or permanent impairing of powers of any member or joint) which applies when the limb is physically present but non-functional. Emasculation (type 1) is specific to castration/removal of male genitalia. The dominant hand makes this more severe clinically but does not change the legal classification.
highYield: The distinction between grievous hurt type 4 (privation/loss of member) and type 5 (impairing powers) is a subtle but tested nuance.
pyqNote: Scenario-based classification into specific types of grievous hurt under Section 320 appears in advanced NEET PG forensic questions.
